steps.text: Follow the emergency response method validation policy (Agency Policy Directive Number FEM-2010-01).

preconditions.text: In emergency response situations (e.g., natural disaster, homeland security).

steps.text: State clearly and completely the measurement process components validated and the combination of procedures composing a method.
Clearly define the purpose of the method (measurement objectives) and the intended use of the data.

Well define and clearly describe the method scope and applicability, including validated components, nature of analytes/matrices, range of analyte levels, known limitations/assumptions, and how parameters meet data quality objectives.
Evaluate Selectivity.
Evaluate Instrument calibration.
Evaluate Bias/Trueness.
Evaluate Precision.
Evaluate Quantitation limits and range.
Evaluate Detection limits.

preconditions.text: The purpose, scope, and applicability must determine the method performance characteristics chosen for study.

steps.text: Investigate the stability of the analyte(s) from the time of sampling through the time of analysis.
Investigate the extract stability.
Validate the analytical method.

preconditions.text: Ensure that proper sample preservation and storage stability studies were performed during method development.

constraints.text: Analytes may be lost through volatilization, sorption, chemical degradation (abiotic reactions), and microbial degradation.
Microorganisms have the potential to degrade target analytes and represent a significant pathway for the fate and destruction of organic compounds.

steps.text: Evaluate selectivity by testing appropriate matrix blanks and fortified (spiked) matrix blanks.
Test a worst-case sample matrix, as defined in the scope of a method, during both intra- and interlaboratory validation studies.
Study actual samples of material containing an undetermined amount of native analyte to evaluate method selectivity.

Use multiple analytical procedures (e.g., sample preparation, wet-chemistry, and/or instrumental analysis procedures) during an intralaboratory validation study to substantiate the selectivity of the method being evaluated.
Thoroughly describe the basis for quantitative selectivity measures if they are used.
Demonstrate the characteristics of a calibration function and justification for a selected calibration model during an intralaboratory method validation study.

preconditions.text: If adequate representations of matrix blanks cannot be obtained or prepared

constraints.text: Matrix blanks should contain the chemical species, other than the analyte(s) of interest, which are reasonably expected to be present in an actual sample.
A combination of procedures or techniques that are based on different measurement principles should be used.

steps.text: Evaluate calibration models by analyzing multiple levels of calibration standards over a selected working range.
Replicate measurements at each calibrant level to evaluate random error and goodness-of-fit.
Demonstrate instrument response to be a first-order linear equation with a y-intercept of zero within error during intralaboratory method validation.
Include calibration study results in a method validation report.
Thoroughly test and justify the calibration approach during an intralaboratory validation study.
Test the calibration approach according to the required/recommended calibration procedure during an interlaboratory validation study.
Thoroughly study, justify, and document the instrument calibration procedure in a method validation report.
Estimate method bias by measuring materials whose composition is reasonably well known, such as reference materials.

preconditions.text: If a method states that an average response factor may be used for calibration over a specified analyte range

steps.text: Evaluate method repeatability and reproducibility at the extremes of the quantitation range and at regulatory levels.
Clearly state the repeatability and reproducibility conditions so that the measures of dispersion can be properly interpreted and evaluated.
Evaluate method trueness and precision at the extremes of the quantitation range.
Verify defined limits (such as those based on specifications for precision) by testing materials containing analyte at the claimed LOQ.

preconditions.text: The quantitation range must include regulatory levels.

constraints.text: A quantitation range must include either a regulatory or other type of action level.

steps.text: Conduct ruggedness testing using experimental designs (such as a fractional factorial design) to examine method performance under minor variations in operating or environmental conditions.
Identify and document critical control points in the method based on analyst observations and ruggedness testing results.

preconditions.text: Ruggedness testing must be completed before initiating an interlaboratory method validation study.

constraints.text: The changes introduced during testing should reflect expected, reasonable variations likely to be encountered in different laboratories.
The fractional factorial experimental design is not intended for method optimization purposes.

steps.text: Specify the conditions changed when making a statement of reproducibility (e.g., principal of measurement, method of measurement, observer, measuring instrument, reference standard, location, conditions of use, time).
When giving a result, make clear whether it refers to the indication, the uncorrected result, the corrected result, and whether several values are averaged.
